features.feature: Command: aggressively clean all managed runtime processes, ports, and dependency containers
features.notes: Blast-all cleanup breadth is covered by lifecycle support tests.
features.current_behavior: Python blast-all works and is tested, but the shell path still carries legacy cleanup breadth for global runtime teardown.
features.missing_python_behavior: Prove and, where needed, close cleanup symmetry for ports, processes, Docker resources, and stale dependency artifacts across mixed failure states.
features.rollout_risk: Global cleanup regressions could leave orphaned processes, ports, or containers across repos.

features.feature: Command: aggressively clean tree-scoped processes and dependency resources before deleting worktrees
features.notes: Blast-worktree cleanup, including legacy resource cleanup, is covered by lifecycle support and worktree action tests.
features.current_behavior: Python blast-worktree deletes trees and cleans most scoped resources, but full cleanup symmetry still relies on shell behavior as the oracle.
features.missing_python_behavior: Close any remaining tree-scoped cleanup gaps for processes, dependency containers, and legacy-named resources, then prove parity through focused lifecycle tests.
features.rollout_risk: Partial tree cleanup can leave stale containers or listeners that later break planning or startup.

features.feature: Command: clear accumulated runtime logs for selected services
features.notes: Clear-logs is covered through state-action/log parity tests.

features.feature: Command: launch or reuse the dedicated Codex tmux session for repo-local workflows
features.notes: Codex tmux session launch and reuse are Python-owned utility flows with dedicated routing and subprocess coverage.

features.feature: Command: stage, commit, and push selected project changes
features.notes: Python commit flows are covered by action CLI/parity tests, including message sourcing and non-interactive cases.
features.current_behavior: Python commit flows work for normal interactive and headless cases, but commit-message sourcing and helper assumptions are still mixed with legacy expectations.
features.missing_python_behavior: Make Python the clear source of truth for commit message discovery, non-interactive failure cases, and pushed-branch reporting.
features.rollout_risk: Edge-case commit flows can still diverge from user expectations if legacy message-resolution behavior is not fully proven.

features.feature: Command: open the configuration wizard or headless config editor
features.notes: Wizard/headless config flows are Python-owned and well covered by config tests.

features.feature: Command: render the interactive dashboard and command loop
features.notes: Python dashboard rendering and command-loop behavior are covered by dashboard rendering and terminal UI tests.
features.current_behavior: Python dashboard is the default and heavily tested, but shell-era UX expectations still influence some interaction and presentation decisions.
features.missing_python_behavior: Codify the remaining operator-facing dashboard behaviors that still rely on historical shell expectations and prove them in focused UI/runtime tests.
features.rollout_risk: Small dashboard regressions are highly visible to operators even when core runtime behavior is correct.

features.feature: Command: inspect the latest generated debug bundle
features.notes: Debug-last is Python-owned and covered by diagnostics tests.

features.feature: Command: create a debug bundle pack from the Python runtime
features.notes: Debug-pack is intentionally Python-only and covered as part of the supported runtime diagnostics path.

features.feature: Command: render the last generated debug report
features.notes: Debug-report is part of the Python-only diagnostics path.

features.feature: Command: delete selected worktree directories after scoped cleanup
features.notes: Delete-worktree flows are Python-owned and exercised by action/planning tests.

features.feature: Command: print readiness and runtime diagnostics
features.notes: Doctor is Python-owned and explicitly covered by runtime command parity tests.
features.current_behavior: Python doctor reports runtime readiness, parity, state health, and recent failure diagnostics without shell migration fields.
features.missing_python_behavior: Keep the doctor output contract focused on runtime readiness and state diagnostics, then cover those fields explicitly in Python tests.
features.rollout_risk: Doctor output drift is mostly a diagnostics/readiness concern, but it can confuse operators during cutover.

features.feature: Command: create or reuse a single envctl-managed worktree without runtime startup
features.notes: Ensure-worktree is Python-owned and intentionally reuses existing planning/worktree creation mechanics without starting runtime services.

features.feature: Command: print current error diagnostics for the latest run
features.notes: Error reporting is covered by runtime truth and dashboard tests.

features.feature: Command: explain startup selection and gating without starting services
features.notes: Explain-startup is Python-owned and verified through env/runtime tests.

features.feature: Command: print current health for services and requirements
features.notes: Health reporting is Python-owned and exercised by runtime truth tests.

features.feature: Command: print runtime help and usage guidance
features.notes: Help and usage guidance are now owned by Python for both the installed CLI and the top-level launcher wrapper.
features.current_behavior: Users can get help successfully, but top-level launcher usage and shell-backed help text still contribute to the final behavior.
features.missing_python_behavior: Make Python the unambiguous source of help/usage semantics while preserving the current user-visible content and examples.
features.rollout_risk: Help text drift creates immediate confusion for installation and first-run workflows.

features.feature: Command: install envctl-managed AI CLI prompt presets into user-local directories
features.notes: Install-prompts is Python-owned and intentionally excluded from dashboard interactive command entry.

features.feature: Command: print the supported command inventory
features.notes: List-commands is explicitly parity-tested against the shell inventory.

features.feature: Command: print available project and service targets
features.notes: List-targets is Python-owned and tested through runtime discovery paths.

features.feature: Command: print available tree targets
features.notes: List-trees is Python-owned and covered by router/runtime tests.

features.feature: Command: tail or follow logs for selected services
features.notes: Logs/follow behavior is covered by runtime parity tests.

features.feature: Command: run project migration actions on selected targets
features.notes: Python migrate dispatch and output semantics are covered by action parity tests.
features.current_behavior: Python migrate dispatch works, but behavior and output expectations are still inherited from legacy helper conventions.
features.missing_python_behavior: Lock down migrate target semantics, output reporting, and helper fallback behavior in Python tests and ownership docs.
features.rollout_risk: Migration actions may remain brittle in mixed helper/native setups without a fully Python-owned contract.

features.feature: Command: migrate legacy shell hook functions into a Python .envctl_hooks.py module
features.notes: Hook migration is Python-owned and provides an explicit path away from executable shell hooks.

features.feature: Command: create or sync planning-driven worktrees and start or attach to the selected environment
features.notes: Plan creation, sync, scale-down, and disabled-run dashboard flows are implemented in Python.

features.feature: Command: create pull requests for selected projects
features.notes: Python PR action flows are covered by CLI/action parity tests across native and helper-backed paths.
features.current_behavior: Python PR creation supports helper and gh-backed paths, but legacy helper assumptions still shape the contract.
features.missing_python_behavior: Finish defining the PR action contract so Python behavior is the source of truth for helper execution, output, and failure handling.
features.rollout_risk: PR workflows may still depend on helper-specific assumptions that are not fully captured in Python tests.

features.feature: Command: emit a versioned startup-preflight contract without starting services
features.notes: Preflight is Python-owned and wraps explain-startup in a versioned JSON contract for automation clients.

features.feature: Command: restart selected services or projects without losing the current run context
features.notes: Restart orchestration and selector behavior are covered in runtime and UI suites.

features.feature: Command: resume the last saved run state for the selected mode
features.notes: Python resume is the current default path and is exercised by state/lifecycle tests.

features.feature: Command: generate a merge/readiness review bundle for selected projects
features.notes: Python review now owns runtime-scoped artifacts, retained files, and output presentation, with CLI/parity coverage.
features.current_behavior: Python review produces runtime-scoped artifacts and improved output, but helper-backed review behavior is still anchored to the legacy analysis helper contract.
features.missing_python_behavior: Define and prove a stable Python-owned review contract for helper output, retained files, and output semantics so shell helper behavior is no longer the oracle.
features.rollout_risk: Review output and retained artifacts can still drift if helper behavior changes independently of Python expectations.

features.feature: Command: destroy the current worktree after scoped cleanup using the active run context
features.notes: Self-destruct-worktree is Python-owned and routes through the action orchestrator using existing scoped cleanup behavior.

features.feature: Command: inspect, attach to, or terminate saved runtime sessions
features.notes: Session inspection and session-management helpers are Python-owned direct inspection flows.

features.feature: Command: print the effective local configuration
features.notes: Config inspection is Python-owned and validated by config/runtime tests.

features.feature: Command: print the latest saved runtime state
features.notes: Show-state is backed by the scoped state repository and covered by state/runtime tests.

features.feature: Command: start main or tree services for the selected mode
features.notes: Core startup flows are owned by Python and covered by runtime/lifecycle parity tests.

features.feature: Command: stop selected services or projects
features.notes: Targeted stop flows are covered by lifecycle parity and cleanup integration tests.

features.feature: Command: stop all managed services for the current runtime scope
features.notes: Python stop-all is covered by runtime parity and end-to-end tests.

features.feature: Command: run tests for selected projects and service scopes
features.notes: Python test execution, progress reporting, and failure summarization are covered by action and streaming fallback tests.
features.current_behavior: Python test execution works across native and helper-backed paths, but the shell runtime still embodies older test-runner edge semantics.
features.missing_python_behavior: Stabilize target selection, helper integration, streaming/progress output, and failure summarization so shell is no longer needed as the fallback oracle.
features.rollout_risk: Users could see confusing test selection or incomplete error propagation in interactive flows.

features.feature: Top-level envctl launcher resolves repo root, honors --repo, and forwards commands into the runtime
features.notes: The top-level launcher now delegates repo resolution, doctor, and forwarding behavior through the Python launcher module.
features.current_behavior: The shell launcher owns repo-root resolution and command forwarding before the Python runtime starts.
features.missing_python_behavior: Decide whether repo resolution should remain a launcher concern or move into Python, and then prove the chosen contract independently of shell behavior.
features.rollout_risk: Launcher regressions can make envctl unusable before Python runtime logic even begins.

features.feature: envctl install writes a shell-file hook block that exposes envctl on PATH
features.notes: Install is now implemented and tested in Python for both the installed CLI and the top-level launcher wrapper.
features.current_behavior: Install is implemented entirely in shell wrapper/installer code and remains outside Python runtime ownership.
features.missing_python_behavior: Define the long-term ownership model for install behavior and prove it with stable tests that do not assume shell runtime semantics.
features.rollout_risk: Install regressions directly affect package adoption and first-run UX.

features.feature: envctl uninstall removes the shell-file hook block cleanly and idempotently
features.notes: Uninstall is now implemented and tested in Python for both the installed CLI and the top-level launcher wrapper.
features.current_behavior: Uninstall is implemented by the shell installer path and has no Python-owned equivalent.
features.missing_python_behavior: Capture uninstall semantics as an explicit contract and prove idempotent removal behavior independent of shell runtime assumptions.
features.rollout_risk: Uninstall drift is less severe than install drift but still affects trust in the package workflow.

features.feature: Python command parser remains aligned with the supported public command and flag surface
features.notes: Parser compatibility is now enforced against the Python-owned public command and documentation surface.

features.feature: Interactive planning selection and count synchronization preserve existing runs while adding or removing trees
features.notes: Planning add/remove/zero-scale synchronization, existing-run reuse, and archive behavior are covered by planning and real-startup tests.
features.current_behavior: Python plan selection handles scale-up/down and existing-run reuse, but these flows are still among the most behaviorally rich parts of the old shell runtime.
features.missing_python_behavior: Exhaustively prove plan selection, run reuse, and interactive handoff semantics across add/remove/zero-scale transitions so shell is no longer needed as a behavior oracle.
features.rollout_risk: Plan-selection regressions create high user confusion because they can restart, remove, or preserve the wrong trees.

features.feature: Worktree setup flags reuse, recreate, and include-existing-worktrees behave consistently across planning workflows
features.notes: Setup-worktree reuse, recreate, and include-existing flag behavior is covered by planning/runtime tests.
features.current_behavior: Setup-worktree and include-existing-worktrees flags are present, but parity evidence is still mostly external and shell-era.
features.missing_python_behavior: Inventory and prove reuse/recreate/include flag semantics in Python planning/worktree tests so setup flows are no longer shell-defined by implication.
features.rollout_risk: Worktree setup flags can be destructive, so parity gaps here risk accidental reuse or recreation of the wrong tree.

features.feature: Dependency startup and cleanup remain symmetric for postgres, redis, supabase, and n8n across startup, stop, blast, and planning scale-down
features.notes: Dependency startup and cleanup symmetry is covered by runtime lifecycle, requirements orchestrator, and adapter parity tests.
features.current_behavior: Python requirement startup works, but the shell stack still represents the older complete behavior for cleanup symmetry across dependency families.
features.missing_python_behavior: Prove symmetric startup/cleanup behavior for every dependency family across startup, restart, blast-all, blast-worktree, and planning scale-down.
features.rollout_risk: Lifecycle asymmetry leaves dependency resources behind and is one of the fastest ways to make future runs unreliable.

features.feature: Supabase lifecycle, project naming, and container cleanup remain stable for long project names and repeated plan cycles
features.notes: Supabase naming, cleanup, and repeated lifecycle behavior are covered by dedicated reliability and lifecycle tests.
features.current_behavior: Python Supabase support is real and tested, but legacy shell behavior is still the broader historical reference for repeated cleanup and naming edge cases.
features.missing_python_behavior: Fully prove long-name naming, stale-container cleanup, and repeated lifecycle behavior without needing shell as the fallback oracle.
features.rollout_risk: Supabase cleanup and naming bugs directly block startup and are expensive for users to diagnose manually.

features.feature: Seed or copy-db-storage behavior for per-tree requirements remains consistent with existing workflows
features.notes: Seed and copy-db-storage behavior is covered by runtime startup and requirements tests.
features.current_behavior: Seed/copy-db flags still carry shell-era expectations and are not yet expressed as a full Python-owned contract.
features.missing_python_behavior: Define the exact retained seeding behavior and cover it with Python and end-to-end tests so the feature is no longer implicitly shell-defined.
features.rollout_risk: Incorrect seeding semantics can corrupt developer expectations and destroy trust in tree isolation.

features.feature: Run state, runtime map, ports manifest, and error report remain the authoritative per-run artifact set
features.notes: Core runtime artifacts are already Python-owned and well covered.

features.feature: Test results and review artifacts live under the scoped runtime /tmp tree and retain valid dashboard links
features.notes: Artifact relocation into runtime scope is Python-owned and covered by focused tests.

features.feature: Artifact retention and cleanup remain correct across stop, blast, resume, and plan scale-down flows
features.notes: Artifact retention and cleanup semantics are covered by state repository and lifecycle parity tests.
features.current_behavior: Python artifacts are scoped correctly, but retention and cleanup semantics across all lifecycle transitions are not yet expressed as a full parity contract.
features.missing_python_behavior: Codify artifact retention expectations for stop, blast, resume, and plan scale-down, then cover them with focused repository and lifecycle tests.
features.rollout_risk: Artifact cleanup regressions can leave stale state that confuses later runs and operator debugging.

features.feature: Release or shipability checks reflect the retained product contract instead of historical shell migration governance
features.notes: Release and cutover gate behavior is Python-owned and covered by release gate and cutover truth tests.
features.current_behavior: Release and doctor readiness checks now use the Python runtime readiness contract and parity manifest as their source of truth.
features.missing_python_behavior: Keep the release gate aligned with runtime readiness and parity freshness, then cover the retained contract directly in Python tests.
features.rollout_risk: This is mainly a developer-workflow risk, but confusing gates slow down confident cutover decisions.
